questions.scenario:
A pre-switch validation report says:

```text
URL tested: https://orders-api.polaris.example/version
task definition: orders-api:41
expected green task definition: orders-api:42
```

The report marks the test as passed because the endpoint returned 200. What is the correct review response?

options.text: Reject the result because the test hit the public blue URL, not the green test listener.
Accept the result because any 200 response proves green is healthy.
Switch traffic because blue and green always share the same task definition before the switch.
Delete blue so the public URL must hit green next time.

questions.explanation: Before the switch, the public URL points to blue. The task definition `orders-api:41` confirms the test did not exercise green. The validation must use the test listener and prove `orders-api:42` answered.

questions.scenario: After a blue-green traffic switch, CodeDeploy reports routing completed. The public `/version` endpoint still reports `orders-api:41`. What is the most likely release concern?

options.text: The production listener may still point at blue or the wrong target group.
This is expected because blue-green always leaves public traffic on blue for one hour.
The version endpoint is never useful after a traffic switch.
The checkout smoke test should be skipped because CodeDeploy already succeeded.

questions.explanation: The platform status is one signal, but the public version endpoint proves what users are actually hitting. If it still reports blue, inspect the deployment and target groups before continuing.

questions.scenario: Green receives 100 percent production traffic and checkout starts returning 500s. Blue is still healthy and compatible with current data. What is the first recovery move?

options.text: Send traffic back to blue, then debug green after users are protected.
Keep traffic on green and inspect logs until root cause is fully known.
Rebuild the image from main and deploy it to green immediately.
Delete green and blue so CodeDeploy recreates both target groups.

questions.explanation: The article frames blue-green rollback as a traffic decision. If blue is still healthy and data-compatible, moving users back to blue protects them first. Deep debugging can happen after the traffic state is safe.

questions.scenario: Green writes a new field named `discountRulesV2`. Blue can only read `discountRules`. Green served traffic for ten minutes before errors started. Why might a traffic rollback to blue be unsafe?

options.text: Blue may fail when it reads records that green already wrote in the new shape.
Traffic rollback always deletes data written by green.
CodeDeploy prevents rollback after ten minutes.
Blue-green deployments cannot be used with databases at all.

questions.explanation: Blue-green can make traffic movement easy, but it does not undo data writes. If green wrote data blue cannot read, rollback can create a second failure. The article points to expand-and-contract compatibility as the safer pattern.

questions.scenario: A team wants to fully warm a replacement Spring service, verify readiness through a private URL, and then make a single production switch. Which deployment style fits best?

options.text: Blue-green, because it prepares a complete replacement before users reach it.
Rolling, because it always gives a private URL for the new version.
Canary, because it never sends real traffic to the new version.
Manual deploy, because automated release patterns cannot handle warmup.

questions.explanation: Blue-green is useful when the team wants to prepare and test a full replacement before moving public traffic. Rolling changes production gradually and usually does not provide a separate pre-switch public path for the full replacement.

questions.scenario: Which checks belong before or immediately after a blue-green traffic switch for `polaris-orders-api`?

options.text: Before switch: test listener `/readyz` returns ready for green.
Before switch: test listener `/version` reports `orders-api:42`.
After switch: public `/version` reports `orders-api:42`.
After switch: public smoke checkout succeeds.
Before switch: public `/version` reports `orders-api:41` and that is accepted as green validation.

questions.explanation: Green must be tested through the test listener before users reach it. After the switch, the public URL should prove users are on green and checkout still works. A public blue response before the switch is not green validation.
